seed_note: Initial issues seeded manually. Awaiting first pipeline run for full scraping.

issues.title: OC Board of Supervisors District 4 — Open Seat Race
issues.summary: District 4 on the Orange County Board of Supervisors is an open seat in the June 2026 primary. The board currently has a 3-2 Democratic majority. If Democrats lose this seat (or District 5), the board flips to Republican majority — affecting the $10.8B annual budget, land use policy, homelessness response, and transportation funding across all of Orange County. This is the single highest-stakes local race in OC for 2026.

public_comment.instructions: This is an election. Register to vote, research candidates, volunteer, donate.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ The Board of Supervisors controls Orange County's $10.8B budget and shapes policy on housing, transit, homelessness, and public safety. The current 3-2 Democratic majority has been more receptive to state housing mandates and transit investment. Losing this seat flips the board — every policy priority shifts. For a moderate Democrat in a purple county, this is where your vote and engagement have maximum leverage. Low-information elections like county supervisor races are exactly where organized civic engagement changes outcomes.

issues.title: State Senate SD-34 — Open Seat (Tom Umberg termed out)
issues.summary: Tom Umberg (D) is termed out of State Senate District 34, creating an open seat. Avelino Valencia (D), the current Assemblymember for AD-68, is the frontrunner. This is Doug's direct state senator race. The state Senate shapes California's housing mandate framework (SB 9, RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy) that drives the core YIMBY fight in Orange County.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research candidates' housing positions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ Your state senator directly influences California's housing mandate framework — the laws that force OC cities to zone for more housing. A senator who strengthens RHNA enforcement and Builder's Remedy makes the local YIMBY fight easier. Valencia's positions on housing should be evaluated carefully. In California, unlike Texas, the state government is the YIMBY ally pushing local governments to build.

issues.title: City of Orange Housing Element Compliance
issues.summary: The California Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) rejected the City of Orange's Housing Element in 2023, finding it non-compliant with state law. This puts the city in 'Builder's Remedy' territory — developers can bypass local zoning restrictions for projects that include affordable units. Multiple Builder's Remedy applications are pending, including the Orange Heights 1,180-unit development. The city is attempting to update its Housing Element to regain compliance, but has faced significant resident opposition to increased density. 87.5% of OC cities made their SB 9 ordinances more restrictive than state law requires.

public_comment.instructions: Attend City of Orange council meetings. Public comment on Housing Element items. Check cityoforange.org for agenda.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ This is the ground zero of the OC YIMBY fight. Unlike Austin where you push for new pro-housing policy, in Orange you're pushing the city to COMPLY with state mandates it's trying to evade. The Housing Element rejection means Builder's Remedy is in effect — developers can build projects with affordable units regardless of local zoning. This is the most direct path to new housing in the city. Supporting compliance and defending Builder's Remedy projects is high-impact advocacy.

issues.title: Irvine General Plan Update — 57,656 Units Proposed
issues.summary: The City of Irvine is updating its General Plan with a proposal to zone for up to 57,656 new housing units. This would be one of the largest zoning capacity increases in Southern California history. The Irvine Company (Donald Bren), which controls ~75% of Irvine's apartment units, is a key stakeholder. The update also involves the Great Park development area. The 5-2 Democratic council majority currently supports the high-density scenario, but November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) could change the dynamics.

public_comment.instructions: Attend Irvine City Council meetings. Note: this is a neighboring city, but its housing decisions affect the entire OC market.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 57,656 units would transform OC's housing landscape. Even though Irvine isn't your city, its housing market directly affects rents and availability across Orange County. The Irvine Company monopoly makes this unique — one company's decisions shape housing for 314,000 people. Supporting the high-density scenario from outside Irvine adds to the pro-housing coalition and sets a precedent for other OC cities.

issues.title: OC Streetcar Launch — First Rail Transit in OC
issues.summary: The OC Streetcar (4.15 miles, connecting Santa Ana to Garden Grove) is scheduled to launch in Spring 2026. This is the first modern rail transit project in Orange County. While modest in scope, it represents a proof-of-concept for rail transit in a car-dominated county. Ridership numbers and public reception will determine whether the system is expanded. Metrolink commuter rail also serves the area but is underutilized.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor launch and ridership data. OCTA board meetings are public.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ For a transit advocate, the OC Streetcar is a small but symbolically important project. If it succeeds, it builds the case for expanded rail transit in OC — connecting more of the county and potentially serving the UCI area. If it fails, it becomes ammunition against future transit investment. Monitor ridership data and expansion proposals. This is a watch item, not an action item.

issues.title: OC Homelessness — Grand Jury Criticism of County Approach
issues.summary: The 2025 Orange County Grand Jury found that the county's approach to homelessness 'remains mostly reactive' rather than preventive. The report criticized fragmented services, insufficient data tracking, and lack of coordination between the county and its 34 cities. Orange County's homeless population is concentrated in Santa Ana and the Santa Ana River corridor. The Board of Supervisors majority (currently 3-2 D) shapes the county's response, making the District 4 and 5 elections directly relevant to this issue.

public_comment.instructions: Board of Supervisors meetings. Written comments accepted via email.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ The Grand Jury critique mirrors the Austin Safe & Sound approach — accountability through data and outcomes tracking. OC's homelessness challenge is different from Austin's (more concentrated, different climate dynamics, different shelter infrastructure) but the same both/and framework applies. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness. The board majority determines whether the county takes a reactive or proactive approach. Connect this to the District 4/5 board races.
